coredata.dc:title: Surface functionality as a key parameter for the conductivity of microwave synthesized CQDs thin films
coredata.dc:creator: Prekodravac, Jovana R.
Budimir, Milica D.
Kleut, Duška N.
Vasiljević, Bojana R.
Rajić, Vladimir B.
Ciasca, Gabriele
Todorović Marković, Biljana M.
coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with extraordinary physicochemical properties have a broad range of applications. Some of the most intriguing is the CQD thin films for electronic devices and membrane nanofiltration. Here we present the simple and affordable microwave-assisted synthesis method for the production of the nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQD) and iron/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (FeN-CQD). The comprehensive study of the morphology and electrical properties of N-CQD and FeN-CQD was discussed after manufacturing obtained nanomaterials in the form of a thin film on different substrates using different deposition procedures. The morphological features of generated thin films altered dramatically when the substrate and nanomaterial properties, as well as the number of deposited layers of CQDs and the chosen deposition process, were taken into consideration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted synthesis
CQDs
Morphology
Electrical properties
Thin films

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The N-CQD and FeN-CQD microwave-assisted synthesis
Substrate surface pretreatment and CQD deposition approaches
Morphological analysis of N-CQD and FeN-CQD thin layers
Electrical conductivity of the N-CQD and FeN-CQD thin layers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra and bandgap evaluation of (A) N-CQD and (B) FeN-CQD samples; FTIR characterization of (C) N-CQD and (D) FeN-CQD.
The N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples EDS analysis (A), the XPS full scan spectra (B, C) and the XPS composition analysis (D), respectively.

AFM morphological and statistical analysis of the spin-coated N-CQD thin films on Mica discs: (A) 20 μl, (B) 40 μl, (C) 60 μl of the deposited sample, and estimated values for (D) mean height, (E) particle diameter, and (F) real particle diameter.
AFM morphological analysis of the drop-casted N-CQD thin films on Si wafers: (A) 20 μl, (B) 40 μl, (C) 60 μl of the deposited sample.

AFM morphological and statistical analysis of the spin-coated FeN-CQD thin films on Mica discs: (A) 20 μl, (B) 40 μl, (C) 60 μl of the deposited sample, and estimated values for (D) mean height, (E) particle diameter, and (F) real particle diameter.
AFM morphological analysis of the drop-casted FeN-CQD thin films on Si wafers: (A) 20 μl, (B) 40 μl, (C) 60 μl of the deposited sample.
Schematic illustration of the thin film formation applying the: (A) drop-casting and (B) spin-coating deposition method.
Sheet resistance and conductivity of 60 μl x-CQDs deposited thin films on Si substrate: (A) N-CQD/Si and (B) FeN-CQD/Si thin films.

head.ce:title: Surface functionality as a key parameter for the conductivity of microwave synthesized CQDs thin films

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fondazione Policlinico Universitario A. Gemelli IRCCS, 00168 Roma, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with extraordinary physicochemical properties have a broad range of applications. Some of the most intriguing is the CQD thin films for electronic devices and membrane nanofiltration. Here we present the simple and affordable microwave-assisted synthesis method for the production of the nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQD) and iron/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (FeN-CQD). The comprehensive study of the morphology and electrical properties of N-CQD and FeN-CQD was discussed after manufacturing obtained nanomaterials in the form of a thin film on different substrates using different deposition procedures. The morphological features of generated thin films altered dramatically when the substrate and nanomaterial properties, as well as the number of deposited layers of CQDs and the chosen deposition process, were taken into consideration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thin film coatings are a widely-explored topic as they are well adapted for the study of physical/optical properties of deposited materials, and have multiple scientific, technological, and commercial applications. A variety of approaches involving various types of precursors have been used to prepare different kinds of thin films. Thin films can be grown on specific substrates through a bottom-up approach [1–3] or deposited using different deposition techniques such as dip-coating, drop-casting or spin-coating [4,5]. Dip-coating or immersion coating is a simple method of depositing a homogeneous thin film onto a substrate by dipping the substrate into the desired sample in a liquid phase. The film thickness is determined by the competition between viscous, capillary or surface tension and gravity forces. The faster the substrate is withdrawn, the thicker the film is deposited [4]. The drop-casting process is commonly used to manufacture the surface of chemically altered electrodes wherein the altering layer is formed of particles such as nanomaterials or nanoparticles and employed for electro-catalysis, particularly for chemical sensors and materials assessment. However, the formation of uniform and homogeneously dispersed thin film surface by this deposition method is limited by materials properties and the drop cast surface, i.e. by the drop size forming the coffee ring effect upon solvent evaporation [5]. Spin-coating is a newly developed method of depositing a homogeneous thin film to various substrates, particularly small flat disks. The process can be divided into four steps involving deposition, spin-up, spin-off, and finally the evaporation step [4].

With the release of a paper by Xu et al. in 2004 on a novel type of fluorescent nanoparticles known as carbon quantum dots (CQD), studies on these carbon nanomaterials have progressed rapidly [6,7]. These innovative low toxicity nanoparticles have optical characteristics similar to their semiconductor equivalents [8,9]. They can be easily synthesized utilizing green low-cost synthesis paths and have been demonstrated to be an outstanding framework for further functionalization and engineering of their chemical nature [10–12]. The hydrothermal and microwave-assisted bottom-up techniques are of special interest since they have proven to be simple, fast and affordable synthesis routes that enable simultaneous synthesis and functionalization of CQD surface chemistry by choosing suitable precursors [13–15]. To date, the CQDs have been used in a form of a suspension, ink, thin film, substrate or as a part of a heterostructure [16]. Solid-state CQDs applications usually involve conjugation and nanocomposite preparation with polymeric matrix in the form of a CQDs thin film deposited on the polymer surface, incorporated into the polymer or as a sandwich between polymer matrices [17,18]. The applications of CQDs and modified CQDs expanded over the years towards imaging, sensing, optoelectronic devices and photochemistry [15,16,19–22]. In recent years, different CQDs have found universal application in the form of a membrane system for nanofiltration, water desalination, and osmotic power production among others [23–31]. The CQDs features, as well as their ease of processing, add value to their potential use in biomedicine as well, particularly as antibacterial and antibiofouling coatings [32–35].

The N-CQD and FeN-CQD nanomaterials were produced by the microwave-assisted method, followed by the investigation of doped CQD nanomaterials thin film formation. The morphological features of produced thin films of N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples on Mica discs and silicon (Si) wafers were then examined, taking into consideration the effect of substrate type, deposition procedure, and deposited sample type. The thin films were deposited using spin-coating and drop-casting techniques, and the homogeneity and uniformity of the deposited thin films were assessed using varied volumes of the deposited material. Finally, the electrical properties of the deposited thin films were calculated by applying the four-point probe method. Due to the diversity of potential application of CQDs in the form of a thin film layer, the investigation of their morphological and electrical properties in presented paper would provide insight into the characteristics and behavior of N-CQD and FeN-CQD in a form of thin layer. As an outcome, it might indicate that they have a promising potential to be used in membrane systems for nanofiltration, among other applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The N-CQD and FeN-CQD microwave-assisted synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: For both sample preparation, the microwave power was set at 100 W for a one-minute reaction. The N-CQD were made from glucose water solution (0.100 g ml−1) in the presence of ammonium hydroxide (25 %), as described in our previous work [36]. The FeN-CQD were prepared by stirring for 2 h the glucose (0.100 g ml−1), urea (0.005 g ml−1) and iron (III) chloride trihydrate (FeCl3 x 3H2O) (0.055 g ml−1) water solution, followed by microwave irradiation under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Substrate surface pretreatment and CQD deposition approaches
ce:section.ce:para: Mica discs are made of Muscovite, which has good dielectric and thermal resistance, chemical inertness, and the ability to produce smooth thin layers, making them ideal as substrates for thin film deposition and AFM characterization. Because of the nature of the substrate, there was no need to clean the Mica discs before deposition. Samples of N-CQD and FeN-CQD at the concentration of 0.5 mg ml−1 were deposited on Mica discs applying the spin-coating method at 3500 rpm spreading 20, 40 or 60 μl of sample to deposit 1 to 3 layers of N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples on a surface of 1 cm2.

The Si wafer surfaces, on the other hand, are frequently contaminated, hence pre-deposition surface cleaning was required. Therefore, the pre-cleaning treatment of the Si wafer (Siegert Wafer, N/Ph, 10 cm2, 1–30 Ω cm−1) was required. The Si wafers were cut to 1 cm2 with a diamond knife and ultrasonically treated for 15 min in the presence of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), a synthetic organic material commonly utilized in the cleaning process, to remove any surface organic contaminants. After that, the substrates were washed numerous times in clean water and ultrasonically treated in the presence of DEMI water. Finally, the substrates were washed in ethanol and dried in an argon environment. The spin-coating deposition approach did not provide the desired material thin film on the Si substrate due to the more hydrophobic properties of the Si wafer surface compared to Mica discs. Because of the tremendous centrifugal force upon spin, the sample tended to sputter from the surface into the atmosphere, leaving only a small amount of the sample on the Si substrate. Therefore, for thin film preparation on the Si wafer surface, the drop-casting approach was more suitable. Using the clean Si substrate, samples of N-CQD and FeN-CQD (0.5 mg ml−1) dots were drop-casted in the same volume as on Mica discs (20, 40 or 60 μl) followed by drying the substrate at 80 °C on a hot plate.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and Energy Dispersive Spectrometry (EDS) were used to examine the structure and elemental content of the samples. XPS analysis of the samples was carried out on SPECS Systems with XP50M X-ray source for Focus 500 and PHOIBOS 100 energy analyzer using a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.74 eV) at 12.5 kV and 12 mA. For the FTIR examination, the thin films of CQD samples were placed on aluminium foil at room temperature. In the spectrum region of 400 to 4000 cm−1, the Nicolet 8700 spectrometer with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 was used to measure the micro ATR FTIR spectra of CQDs thin films. Chemical composition was analyzed by EDS technique using FEI SCIOS 2 Dual Beam microscope. The powder was pressed into a copper double-sided adhesive tape and then recorded under a high vacuum with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. The optical characteristics of the produced N–CQD and FeN–CQD nanomaterials were investigated using UV–Vis absorption spectrum measurements. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of N–CQD and FeN–CQD (1 mg ml−1) dispersed in water were recorded using LLG-uniSPEC 2 spectrophotometer. Spectra were recorded in quartz cuvettes at room temperature in the wavelength range of 220–600 nm. Applying Tauc's plot equation, the bandgap energies were derived from absorption spectra obtained for N–CQD and FeN–CQD samples:
(1)
αhν
1 / n
= A
hν −
E g
A - is a proportional constant obtained through the slope of the linear part of the graphs,
α - is a measured optical absorption coefficient,
Eg - is the bandgap energy of the material and hν is the photon energy obtained by dividing 1243 by incident wavelength,
n - depends on the nature of the optical transition.

The surface morphologies of the deposited thin layers of CQDs were investigated with Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) on Mica discs and Si wafer substrates. Images were obtained at 2 Hz, with 512 × 512 pixels image definition over different square areas. The average size of objects in AFM images and size distribution were determined by Gwyddion software evaluating around 100 quantum dots in each sample. Considering the diameter of the AFM tip and the larger dimensions of particles, the real particle diameter was calculated from the equation previously established in the literature [37]:
(2)
r c
= r
cos
θ 0
+
cos 2
θ 0
+
1 + sin
θ 0
− 1 +
tan
θ 0
cos
θ 0
+
tan 2
θ 0
r - is the particle radius,
θ 0 - is the mean half-angle of the tip,
r
c - is the AFM radius of a particle.
The electrical properties of the deposited thin layers were examined using a four-point probe measuring device over a 1 cm2 sample surface at different points.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to explore the optical characteristics of various doped CQDs in aqueous media. From Fig. 1 A, and B, one can observe the absorption in the UV range with a tail extending towards the visible region. Absorption of the N-CQD samples showed strong absorption at 220, 272 and a small shoulder peak at 310 nm (Fig. 1A).

The absorption at 220 nm corresponds to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains, while the second one at 272 nm relates to the n-π* transition peaks of CO bonds or other linked groups. When comparing these data to the UV–Vis spectra of the pristine CQD, a new band at around 310 nm was observed, which was attributed to the conjugated C–N/C-N bond transition. In the presence of a Fe dopant, the excited defect surface states caused by FeN heteroatom co-doping appeared to cause a broad absorption in the range of 260–350 nm (Fig. 1B). The centre of the n-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains is around 335 nm, indicating the presence of aromatic structure in the FeN-CQD sample. Using Tauc's plots the bandgap energies were extracted from the absorption spectra of N-CQD and FeN-CQD (Fig. 1A, B (inset)). The calculated bandgap energies between the valence and conduction band were 2.8 eV and 2.6 eV for N-CQD and FeN–CQD samples, respectively. The FTIR spectra for both samples (Fig. 1C, D) showed several characteristic peaks confirming the presence of the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H (3200–3400 cm−1), CH (~2930 cm−1) and CC (~1630 cm−1) [36,38]. The presence of amide CN bonds due to the N doping of CQD was confirmed by peaks at 1384 and 1373 cm−1 for the N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples, respectively, whereas CO stretching vibrations of the carboxylic group were found at 1040 cm−1. FTIR spectra of FeN-CQD samples also showed minor intensity peaks at around 600 cm−1, indicating the presence of Fe-O/Fe-N bonds (Fig. 1D) [39]. Obtained data confirmed the presence of hydrophilic oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl, which are responsible for good water solubility and stability of N-CQD and FeN-CQD. In addition, the data verified the implementation of certain dopants (N and FeN) into CQDs [38].

According to the elemental EDS composition analysis of N-CQD (Fig. 2A), samples consist solely of three peaks (C, O and N). On the contrary, the elemental analysis of FeN-CQDs samples apart from C, O and N peaks indicated the presence of Fe. Obtained results implied that the produced N-CQDs and FeN-CQDs have no elemental signature of any foreign contaminants other than C, O, N and Fe. The at.% of the N atom varied depending on whether or not Fe is present in the sample, which could also be observed for C and O atoms. Obtained results suggest the possible Fe incorporation into the CQDs framework resulting in competition/coordination with N atoms. Nonetheless, the elemental analysis was performed on the edge of drop-casted samples on Si substrate, therefore EDS's entire purpose was to ensure that the CQD samples included no foreign trace elements other than the ones expected. The XPS analysis, as a surface-sensitive technique, confirmed the successful doping of CQDs revealing however the lower at.% of N and Fe in N-CQD and FeN-CQD samples as presented in Fig. 2B. The N incorporation into the N- doped CQDs was predominantly in the form of pyridinic-N (64.6 at.%), pyrrolic-N (23.4 at.%) and graphitic-N (12.0 at.%) [15]. The FeN-CQD samples, due to the presence of Fe (2.0 at.%), showed a decrease in N at.% (6.7 at.%) compared to the N-CQD samples, with N atoms predominantly present in the form of pyrrolic-N (58.4 at.%) followed by pyridinic-N (32.0 at.%) and graphitic-N (9.6 at.%). In the FeN co-doped samples, the Fe is most likely coordinated with pyrrolic and pyridinic nitrogen atoms forming FeN active sites as explained elsewhere [39,40]. Obtained EDS and XPS results demonstrated that both N and Fe were successfully doped into CQDs particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of N-CQD and FeN-CQD thin layers
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of N-CQD and FeN-CQD thin films deposited on Mica discs and Si wafers was studied using AFM microscopy. The homogeneity, uniformity, and root mean square (RMS) roughness of deposited thin films were examined concerning the quantity of deposited x-CQD films measured in volume units (μl).

AFM images of N-CQD samples (20, 40, and 60 μl) deposited on Mica discs using a spin-coating deposition approach are shown in Fig. 3 . Due to the tendency of N-CQD to somewhat agglomerate at higher concentrations, samples deposited on Mica discs for AFM characterization were diluted 20 times from their initial concentration. As a result, the dilution allowed the observation of spherical like shapes of N-CQD dots, as shown in Fig. 3 A-C at 5 μm2 AFM images of N-CQD thin films. On the contrary, the high height/diameter flat objects were observed for N-CQD original concentration, confirming the N-CQD agglomeration and overlapping during spin-coating deposition. Increasing the volume of the deposited N-CQD from 20 to 60 μl confirmed the formation of the more uniform thin films with RMS ranging from 1 nm to 1.5 nm and 1.7 nm, respectively. The surface morphology and RMS of the clean Mica disc (0.13 nm) are presented in Fig. S1.

The histogram representing the average mean height of over 100 analyzed N-CQD dots was found to be between 2 and 10 nm, with over 80 % of the height of the dot falling between 2 and 6 nm (Fig. 3D). The diameters of the particles were estimated to be between 20 and 69 nm, with more than 75 % of the dots having dimensions less than 40 nm (Fig. 3E). However, due to the AFM's susceptibility to external influences, the real particle diameter calculation yielded more realistic data for the diameter of the dot, with the maximum percentage of produced dots having real particle diameters in the 15 to 30 nm range (Fig. 3F).

By using the spin-coating approach, the centrifugal force acting on rotation, sputtered the sample equally, allowing the deposition of homogeneous thin layers on a flat surface such as Mica discs. On the other hand, due to the more hydrophobic qualities and higher RMS of a clean Si surface (Fig. S1) up to 0.75 nm compared to Mica discs, the spin-coating approach indicated a low degree of nanomaterial thin film deposition on Si wafer under rotation. For Si surfaces, the drop-casting approach was more efficient, allowing nanomaterials to remain on the substrate after deposition. The N-CQD samples were drop-casted on a 1 cm2 Si wafer, at the same sample concentration and volume (20, 40 60 μl) as on Mica discs, dried on a hot plate and examined by AFM (Fig. 4 A-C). The resulting films were thicker and more focused on a smaller circle area constrained by the drop size when compared to Mica discs samples.

The initial observation after drop-casting 20 μl of N-CQD (Fig. 4A) was a much denser thin film with higher RMS (2 nm), compared to the thin films deposited using spin-coating on Mica discs (Fig. 3A). The N-CQD thin film showed an increase in the measured RMS values (2.5 nm) with flattened morphological dots after drop-casting 40 μl (Fig. 4B). Due to a lack of centrifugal force that would evenly spread the sample as in the case of the spin-coating technique, aggregation and overlapping of N-CQD occurred. Drop-casting the additional 20 μl of the sample (60 μl in total) resulted in the production of an abundant thin film of agglomerated N-CQD on small concentrated surfaces, pointing to the formation of a non-uniform film (Fig. 4C). Therefore, when paralleled to the identical thin films spin-coated on Mica discs, the observed RMS values of N-CQD on the Si surface were two or more times higher due to the grater Si substrate RMS (1 nm) and lower deposited films uniformity.

The FeN-CQD samples were initially more easily deposited on Mica discs at their original concentration, without dilution as opposite to the N-CQD samples. In comparison to N-CQD samples, more dense and uniform thin films were detected after the deposition of only 20 μl of the FeN-CQD sample (Fig. 5A). The measured RMS value for the 20 μl FeN-CQD sample was 1 nm. As the volume of the deposited material increased, the uniformity and homogeneity of thin films improved (Fig. 5B, C), resulting in lower RMS values when compared to the N-CQD. The average mean height of over 100 studied FeN-CQD dots was found to be between 2 and 7 nm, with over 75 % of the dot height ranging between 2 and 6 nm (Fig. 5D). The particle diameters were assessed to be between 20 and 60 nm, with more than 80 % of the dots being smaller than 40 nm in size (Fig. 5E). The real particle diameter estimation, however, revealed more realistic data for the diameter of the dot, with the largest percentage of created dots having real particle diameters in the 15 to 30 nm range (Fig. 5F).

Applying the same deposition conditions, samples FeN-CQD were drop-casted onto the Si surface (Fig. 6 ). Compared to the N-CQD sample deposition in the volume of 20 μl (Fig. 4A), a denser film for the same volume was observed for the FeN-CQD sample (Fig. 6A). Further volume increase led to aggregation and overlapping of deposited dots resulting in abundant films of agglomerated FeN-CQD on small concentrated surfaces (Fig. 6B, C).

Drop-casting, spreading a nanoparticle dispersion across a substrate and allowing it to dry under controlled settings, such as pressure and temperature, is an easy and controllable deposition process for small surfaces (1 cm2). In theory, the thickness of a film is determined by the volume of dispersion utilized and the particle concentration, which are both easily adjustable. Other factors that influence the film structure include how well the solvent wets the substrate, the degree of evaporation, drying capillary forces, and so on. Drop-casting, as demonstrated above provided a quick and easy way to make dense thin films of N-CQD (Fig. 4) and FeN-CQD (Fig. 6) samples on small substrates [5]. Nevertheless, drop-casting has the disadvantage that even under near-ideal settings, variations in film thickness or internal structure might occur due to differences in evaporation rates over the substrate or concentration oscillations (Fig. 7A).

When compared to drop-casting, spin-coating frequently produces more consistent film thicknesses throughout the substrate and can support considerably larger substrates. A substrate is rotated at a high speed while a volume of material, with a known particle concentration, is injected into the center point of the substrate. Centrifugal force causes homogeneous dispersion across the substrate, followed by solvent evaporation and the formation of a thin particle film (Fig. 7B). The variation in the N-CQD (Fig. 3) and FeN-CQD (Fig. 5) dispersion volume (20, 40, 60 μl), while keeping the sample concentration (0.5 mg ml−1) and rotational speeds (3500 rpm) constant, influenced the thickness, homogeneity and density of the produced films.

The DLS data (Fig. S2) revealed that the FeN-CQD particle distribution was higher and more narrowed when compared to the N-CQD widespread particle distribution. However, as an intensity-based method the DLS examines the particle's hydrodynamic diameter, taking into account the salvation layer as well, while AFM measurements only disclose the size of the particle's core because the sample is deposited and in a steady state. The higher agglomeration rate with increase in material concentration was noticed for FeN-CQD when compared to N-CQD. The features of the particle-liquid interface strongly influence the physical characteristics of suspensions and colloids, therefore knowing the zeta potential is helpful. The measured values of Z potential for FeN-CQD (21.4 ± 0.7 mV) and N-CQD (22.5 ± 1.0 mV) indicate a slightly higher agglomeration potential for FeN-CQD samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical conductivity of the N-CQD and FeN-CQD thin layers
ce:section.ce:para: For the thicknesses of x-CQD thin films, the 60 μl of N-CQD and FeN-CQD were deposited on Si wafers and measured quickly and easily using Talystep equipment. A four-point probe measuring device, which is commonly used for bulk or thin films samples, was utilized to examine the electrical properties of the x-CQD thin films.

Because the samples were evaluated as thin films, the sheet resistance (Rs) expressed in ohms per square (Ω/◻) was determined by the equation, calculated by the four-point probe device and confirmed by calculations as well:
(3)
Rs =
π
ln
2
x
U I
Rs - stands for sheet resistance,
I (A) – stands for applied electrical current,
U (V) – stands for the measured voltage.

Talystep equipment was used to measure the thicknesses of N-CQD thin layers at multiple positions along the deposited sample edge. The thickness measurements for N-CQD samples in their original concentration resulted in thin film development from 900 to 1500 nm lengthwise of the measurement spot. The aggregation of N-CQD samples at their original concentration was once again validated by the link between sample diameter/height and calculated Talystep thickness values. The FeN-CQD samples, on the contrary, revealed values of 350 to 500 nm, due to the higher uniformity of the FeN-CQD deposited films compared to N-CQD, which resulted in thin film development and lower thickness value.

Calculations of resistivity (ρ) of a given material can be done using the relation: Rs = resistivity/thickness, implying:
(4)
ρ
Ωm
= Rs ∗ d
For the calculations of resistivity of thin films using the equation above the thickness (d) of the N-CQD samples was estimated as 1200 nm, while for FeN-CQD samples the value was averaged to 425 nm.

Since the conductivity of thin films is reciprocal to resistivity, the relationship between resistivity and conductivity was expressed as:
(5)
σ
S / m
=
1 ρ

The electrical properties were measured for thin films obtained by deposing 60 μl of N-CQD or FeN-CQD samples on Si wafer clean surface, using the four-point probe measuring device and presented in Fig. 8 with (a standard deviation of 5 %). For the N-CQD thin film, measurements began with a current of 10 nA and Rs of 26 kΩ/◻ (Fig. 8A). As the applied current increased sequentially up to the μA scale, for 1 μA current the Rs immediately reduced to 370 Ω/◻. The further current increase had less and less influence on Rs up to a few dozen of μA after what it did not have any significant effect on Rs up to a current of 80 μA when it reached the mΩ/◻ scale. Therefore, for currents less than 80 μA, the N-CQD thin layers Rs were measured in Ω/◻ (~20 Ω/◻), while currents more than 80 μA switched the Rs to an mΩ/◻ scale.

For FeN-CQD/Si thin films (60 μl on Si substrate), experiments were started at 1 μA current showing Rs of 12,000 Ω/◻ (Fig. 8B). The applied current gradual increase of up to several dozen of μA showed a tremendous decrease in Rs, where for 150 μA current, it reached 134 Ω/◻. Further current increase to several hundreds of μA did not cause significant changes, whereas at currents of 300 μA the Rs reached the mΩ/◻ scale. Hence, for currents less than 300 μA, the Rs were on a Ω/◻ scale, while over 300 μA it reaches an mΩ/◻ scale. At set currents, both thin film samples demonstrated good conductivity. While a current of 300 μA produced conductivity of 2500 S/m for the FeN-CQD sample, with steady increase with the applied current, a current of 40 μA was necessary for the N-CQD sample to reach its maximum conductivity of approximately 9000 S/m.

The study presented here demonstrates a straightforward microwave-assisted approach for making N-CQD and FeN-CQD nanomaterials. The obtained N-CQD had a high atomic percentage of doped N, mostly in the form of pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic N incorporated into the CQDs framework. The elemental analysis of FeN-CQD revealed a decrease in N at.% after introducing Fe (2 at.%) through coordination with pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen. The obtained at.% of introduced Fe is among the highest reported atomic percentages according to available literature data. Examining the morphological properties, we concluded that regardless of the type or amount of deposited materials on Mica discs by the spin-coating technique, the preparation of homogeneous thin films was achieved. By increasing the volume of deposited material denser and homogeneous thin films were produced. Due to the more hydrophobic properties of the Si substrate, the drop-casting approach was more appropriate for thin film deposition on the Si wafer surface, revealing the material aggregation at greater concentrations of deposited CQDs, impacting the resulting thin film RMS. The x–CQD thin films morphology was significantly influenced by film thickness, which can be adjusted by the concentration of the depositing solution. Both thin film samples demonstrated good conductivity, continuously rising with the applied current.
Jovana R Prekodravac – Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing.
Milica D Budimir – Visualization; Software;
Duška N Kleut – Data curation, Formal analysis;
Bojana R Vasiljević – Synthesis.
Vladimir B Rajić – Data curation.
Biljana M Todorović Marković– Project administration, Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that there are no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Science Fund of the Republic of Serbia, #7741955, Are photoactive nanoparticles salvation for global infectional treath? - PHOTOGUN4MICROBES and by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia grant number 451-03-68/2022-14/200017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.109366.
